user_query:
I'm leading a beginner-friendly map-and-compass session in the Blue Hills near Boston and want a screen-free navigation training packet that my co-leader can use without me present. Read input/navigation_brief.json for my exact location context, declination, grid system, pace count, and three practice legs given as UTM coordinates. Then produce two deliverables:

1) Write output/route_card.json with:
   - location (string) copied from input
   - declination_deg_east_positive (number) copied from input
   - grid_system (string) copied from input
   - assume_grid_equals_true_north (boolean) copied from input
   - pace_count_per_100m (number) copied from input
   - legs: an array entry for each leg in the input, each containing:
     • from_name and to_name (strings)
     • from and to objects with easting and northing (numbers, mirror the input)
     • grid_bearing_deg: azimuth from grid north in degrees (0–360), computed as atan2(ΔE, ΔN) converted to degrees, normalized to [0, 360)
     • magnetic_bearing_deg: grid_bearing adjusted by declination (east is positive). Normalize to [0, 360)
     • distance_m: straight-line distance in meters between points (Pythagorean), round to 1 decimal
     • estimated_paces: distance_m * (pace_count_per_100m / 100), round to nearest whole number
     • handrail (string): a plausible linear feature to follow for that leg
     • backstop (string): a plausible “you went too far if …” feature for that leg
   Use the exact declination value from the input. Assume grid north equals true north for this exercise (no meridian convergence correction). Round grid_bearing_deg and magnetic_bearing_deg to 1 decimal place.

2) Write output/training_plan.md as a concise, step-by-step mini-course tailored to Blue Hills/Boston beginners that a co-leader can read aloud. Include:
   - Map reading fundamentals (legend, scale, contour lines, grid references) adapted to the grid system in the input
   - Compass basics with an explicit sentence stating the local magnetic declination value from the input and how it’s applied to bearings
   - A clear, numbered procedure for taking a bearing from the map and following it in the field
   - Terrain association guidance including handrails and backstops, plus simple distance estimation (pace counting) using the provided pace count
   - Natural direction methods: the shadow stick method and at least one night-sky method (Polaris or Southern Cross, whichever fits your choice, but explain applicability)
   - The STOP protocol (Sit, Think, Observe, Plan) with brief, actionable sentences for each step
   - A short city-without-GPS section (directions by cardinal points and permanent landmarks)
   - A safety note that explicitly tells people to carry a charged phone for emergencies and to tell someone their route and return time; include the emergency number they should use in the US
   - A 30–60 minute practice exercise that uses the three provided UTM legs: orient the map, compute bearings, follow a leg, identify handrails/backstops, estimate distance by paces, and verify position

Keep everything plain text and write files only under output/.
